1→# Database Models for Mochi Donut - SQLAlchemy 2.0 Async
     2→"""
     3→Production-ready SQLAlchemy 2.0 models with async patterns for the Mochi Donut
     4→spaced repetition learning system. Follows FastAPI best practices and includes
     5→proper indexing for performance optimization.
     6→"""
     7→
     8→from datetime import datetime
     9→from enum import Enum as PyEnum
    10→from typing import List, Optional
    11→import uuid
    12→
    13→from sqlalchemy import (
    14→    JSON, Boolean, DateTime, Enum, Float, ForeignKey, Index, String, Text,
    15→    UniqueConstraint, func
    16→)
    17→from sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import UUID
    18→from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncAttrs
    19→from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column, relationship
    20→
    21→
    22→class Base(AsyncAttrs, DeclarativeBase):
    23→    """Base class for all database models with async support."""
    24→    pass
    25→
    26→
    27→# Enums for type safety
    28→class SourceType(PyEnum):
    29→    """Content source types supported by the system."""
    30→    WEB = "web"
    31→    PDF = "pdf"
    32→    YOUTUBE = "youtube"
    33→    NOTION = "notion"
    34→    RAINDROP = "raindrop"
    35→    MARKDOWN = "markdown"
    36→
    37→
    38→class PromptType(PyEnum):
    39→    """Types of prompts following Andy Matuschak's patterns."""
    40→    FACTUAL = "factual"
    41→    PROCEDURAL = "procedural"
    42→    CONCEPTUAL = "conceptual"
    43→    OPEN_LIST = "open_list"
    44→    CLOZE_DELETION = "cloze_deletion"
    45→
    46→
    47→class ProcessingStatus(PyEnum):
    48→    """Status of content processing through the system."""
    49→    PENDING = "pending"
    50→    PROCESSING = "processing"
    51→    COMPLETED = "completed"
    52→    FAILED = "failed"
    53→    SKIPPED = "skipped"
    54→
    55→
    56→class QualityMetricType(PyEnum):
    57→    """Quality metrics for prompt evaluation."""
    58→    FOCUS_SPECIFICITY = "focus_specificity"
    59→    PRECISION_CLARITY = "precision_clarity"
    60→    COGNITIVE_LOAD = "cognitive_load"
    61→    RETRIEVAL_PRACTICE = "retrieval_practice"
    62→    OVERALL_QUALITY = "overall_quality"
    63→
    64→
    65→class AgentType(PyEnum):
    66→    """Types of AI agents in the system."""
    67→    ORCHESTRATOR = "orchestrator"
    68→    CONTENT_ANALYSIS = "content_analysis"
    69→    PROMPT_GENERATION = "prompt_generation"
    70→    QUALITY_REVIEW = "quality_review"
    71→    REFINEMENT = "refinement"
    72→
    73→
    74→# Core Models
    75→
    76→class Content(Base):
    77→    """
    78→    Stores processed content from various sources with unified markdown format.
    79→    Includes metadata for semantic search and duplicate detection.
    80→    """
    81→    __tablename__ = "contents"
    82→
    83→    # Primary key and identification
    84→    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
    85→        UUID(as_uuid=True),
    86→        primary_key=True,
    87→        default=uuid.uuid4,
    88→        index=True
    89→    )
    90→
    91→    # Source information
    92→    source_url: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(2048), nullable=True)
    93→    source_type: Mapped[SourceType] = mapped_column(Enum(SourceType), nullable=False)
    94→    title: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(500), nullable=True)
    95→    author: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
    96→
    97→    # Content storage
    98→    raw_text: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
    99→    markdown_content: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=False)
   100→    content_hash: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64), nullable=False, unique=True)
   101→
   102→    # Vector database integration
   103→    chroma_collection: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
   104→    chroma_document_id: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
   105→
   106→    # Processing metadata
   107→    word_count: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)
   108→    estimated_reading_time: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)  # minutes
   109→    processing_status: Mapped[ProcessingStatus] = mapped_column(
   110→        Enum(ProcessingStatus),
   111→        default=ProcessingStatus.PENDING
   112→    )
   113→
   114→    # Metadata and configuration
   115→    content_metadata: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
   116→    processing_config: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
   117→
   118→    # Timestamps
   119→    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
   120→        DateTime(timezone=True),
   121→        server_default=func.now()
   122→    )
   123→    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
   124→        DateTime(timezone=True),
   125→        server_default=func.now(),
   126→        onupdate=func.now()
   127→    )
   128→    processed_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
   129→        DateTime(timezone=True),
   130→        nullable=True
   131→    )
   132→
   133→    # Relationships
   134→    prompts: Mapped[List["Prompt"]] = relationship(
   135→        "Prompt",
   136→        back_populates="content",
   137→        cascade="all, delete-orphan"
   138→    )
   139→    agent_executions: Mapped[List["AgentExecution"]] = relationship(
   140→        "AgentExecution",
   141→        back_populates="content",
   142→        cascade="all, delete-orphan"
   143→    )
   144→
   145→    # Indexes for performance
   146→    __table_args__ = (
   147→        Index("ix_content_source_type", "source_type"),
   148→        Index("ix_content_status", "processing_status"),
   149→        Index("ix_content_created_at", "created_at"),
   150→        Index("ix_content_hash", "content_hash"),
   151→        Index("ix_content_chroma", "chroma_collection", "chroma_document_id"),
   152→    )
   153→
   154→
   155→class Prompt(Base):
   156→    """
   157→    Generated flashcard prompts with quality metrics and versioning.
   158→    Tracks relationship to source content and Mochi integration.
   159→    """
   160→    __tablename__ = "prompts"
   161→
   162→    # Primary key
   163→    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
   164→        UUID(as_uuid=True),
   165→        primary_key=True,
   166→        default=uuid.uuid4,
   167→        index=True
   168→    )
   169→
   170→    # Content relationship
   171→    content_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
   172→        UUID(as_uuid=True),
   173→        ForeignKey("contents.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
   174→        nullable=False
   175→    )
   176→
   177→    # Prompt content
   178→    question: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=False)
   179→    answer: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=False)
   180→    prompt_type: Mapped[PromptType] = mapped_column(Enum(PromptType), nullable=False)
   181→
   182→    # Quality and confidence
   183→    confidence_score: Mapped[Optional[float]] = mapped_column(Float, nullable=True)
   184→    difficulty_level: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)  # 1-5 scale
   185→
   186→    # Versioning and editing
   187→    version: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=1, nullable=False)
   188→    is_edited: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(Boolean, default=False)
   189→    edit_reason: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(500), nullable=True)
   190→
   191→    # Mochi integration
   192→    mochi_card_id: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
   193→    mochi_deck_id: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
   194→    mochi_status: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(50), nullable=True)
   195→
   196→    # Context and metadata
   197→    source_context: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
   198→    tags: Mapped[Optional[List[str]]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
   199→    prompt_metadata: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
   200→
   201→    # Timestamps
   202→    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
   203→        DateTime(timezone=True),
   204→        server_default=func.now()
   205→    )
   206→    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(

# Database Models for Mochi Donut - SQLAlchemy 2.0 Async
"""
Production-ready SQLAlchemy 2.0 models with async patterns for the Mochi Donut
spaced repetition learning system. Follows FastAPI best practices and includes
proper indexing for performance optimization.
"""

from datetime import datetime
from enum import Enum as PyEnum
from typing import List, Optional
import uuid

from sqlalchemy import (
    JSON, Boolean, DateTime, Enum, Float, ForeignKey, Index, String, Text,
    UniqueConstraint, func
)
from sqlalchemy.dialects.postgresql import UUID
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncAttrs
from sqlalchemy.orm import DeclarativeBase, Mapped, mapped_column, relationship


class Base(AsyncAttrs, DeclarativeBase):
    """Base class for all database models with async support."""
    pass


# Enums for type safety
class SourceType(PyEnum):
    """Content source types supported by the system."""
    WEB = "web"
    PDF = "pdf"
    YOUTUBE = "youtube"
    NOTION = "notion"
    RAINDROP = "raindrop"
    MARKDOWN = "markdown"


class PromptType(PyEnum):
    """Types of prompts following Andy Matuschak's patterns."""
    FACTUAL = "factual"
    PROCEDURAL = "procedural"
    CONCEPTUAL = "conceptual"
    OPEN_LIST = "open_list"
    CLOZE_DELETION = "cloze_deletion"


class ProcessingStatus(PyEnum):
    """Status of content processing through the system."""
    PENDING = "pending"
    PROCESSING = "processing"
    COMPLETED = "completed"
    FAILED = "failed"
    SKIPPED = "skipped"


class QualityMetricType(PyEnum):
    """Quality metrics for prompt evaluation."""
    FOCUS_SPECIFICITY = "focus_specificity"
    PRECISION_CLARITY = "precision_clarity"
    COGNITIVE_LOAD = "cognitive_load"
    RETRIEVAL_PRACTICE = "retrieval_practice"
    OVERALL_QUALITY = "overall_quality"


class AgentType(PyEnum):
    """Types of AI agents in the system."""
    ORCHESTRATOR = "orchestrator"
    CONTENT_ANALYSIS = "content_analysis"
    PROMPT_GENERATION = "prompt_generation"
    QUALITY_REVIEW = "quality_review"
    REFINEMENT = "refinement"


# Core Models

class Content(Base):
    """
    Stores processed content from various sources with unified markdown format.
    Includes metadata for semantic search and duplicate detection.
    """
    __tablename__ = "contents"

    # Primary key and identification
    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        primary_key=True,
        default=uuid.uuid4,
        index=True
    )

    # Source information
    source_url: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(2048), nullable=True)
    source_type: Mapped[SourceType] = mapped_column(Enum(SourceType), nullable=False)
    title: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(500), nullable=True)
    author: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)

    # Content storage
    raw_text: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
    markdown_content: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=False)
    content_hash: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(64), nullable=False, unique=True)

    # Vector database integration
    chroma_collection: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
    chroma_document_id: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)

    # Processing metadata
    word_count: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)
    estimated_reading_time: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)  # minutes
    processing_status: Mapped[ProcessingStatus] = mapped_column(
        Enum(ProcessingStatus),
        default=ProcessingStatus.PENDING
    )

    # Metadata and configuration
    content_metadata: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
    processing_config: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)

    # Timestamps
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now()
    )
    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now(),
        onupdate=func.now()
    )
    processed_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True
    )

    # Relationships
    prompts: Mapped[List["Prompt"]] = relationship(
        "Prompt",
        back_populates="content",
        cascade="all, delete-orphan"
    )
    agent_executions: Mapped[List["AgentExecution"]] = relationship(
        "AgentExecution",
        back_populates="content",
        cascade="all, delete-orphan"
    )

    # Indexes for performance
    __table_args__ = (
        Index("ix_content_source_type", "source_type"),
        Index("ix_content_status", "processing_status"),
        Index("ix_content_created_at", "created_at"),
        Index("ix_content_hash", "content_hash"),
        Index("ix_content_chroma", "chroma_collection", "chroma_document_id"),
    )


class Prompt(Base):
    """
    Generated flashcard prompts with quality metrics and versioning.
    Tracks relationship to source content and Mochi integration.
    """
    __tablename__ = "prompts"

    # Primary key
    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        primary_key=True,
        default=uuid.uuid4,
        index=True
    )

    # Content relationship
    content_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        ForeignKey("contents.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
        nullable=False
    )

    # Prompt content
    question: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=False)
    answer: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=False)
    prompt_type: Mapped[PromptType] = mapped_column(Enum(PromptType), nullable=False)

    # Quality and confidence
    confidence_score: Mapped[Optional[float]] = mapped_column(Float, nullable=True)
    difficulty_level: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)  # 1-5 scale

    # Versioning and editing
    version: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=1, nullable=False)
    is_edited: Mapped[bool] = mapped_column(Boolean, default=False)
    edit_reason: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(500), nullable=True)

    # Mochi integration
    mochi_card_id: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
    mochi_deck_id: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
    mochi_status: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(50), nullable=True)

    # Context and metadata
    source_context: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
    tags: Mapped[Optional[List[str]]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
    prompt_metadata: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)

    # Timestamps
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now()
    )
    updated_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now(),
        onupdate=func.now()
    )
    edited_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True
    )
    sent_to_mochi_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True
    )

    # Relationships
    content: Mapped["Content"] = relationship("Content", back_populates="prompts")
    quality_metrics: Mapped[List["QualityMetric"]] = relationship(
        "QualityMetric",
        back_populates="prompt",
        cascade="all, delete-orphan"
    )
    user_interactions: Mapped[List["UserInteraction"]] = relationship(
        "UserInteraction",
        back_populates="prompt",
        cascade="all, delete-orphan"
    )

    # Indexes for performance
    __table_args__ = (
        Index("ix_prompt_content_id", "content_id"),
        Index("ix_prompt_type", "prompt_type"),
        Index("ix_prompt_confidence", "confidence_score"),
        Index("ix_prompt_mochi_card", "mochi_card_id"),
        Index("ix_prompt_created_at", "created_at"),
        Index("ix_prompt_content_type", "content_id", "prompt_type"),
    )


class QualityMetric(Base):
    """
    Quality assessment metrics for prompts based on Andy Matuschak's principles.
    Tracks multiple quality dimensions and feedback from LLM judges.
    """
    __tablename__ = "quality_metrics"

    # Primary key
    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        primary_key=True,
        default=uuid.uuid4
    )

    # Prompt relationship
    prompt_id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        ForeignKey("prompts.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
        nullable=False
    )

    # Metric details
    metric_type: Mapped[QualityMetricType] = mapped_column(
        Enum(QualityMetricType),
        nullable=False
    )
    score: Mapped[float] = mapped_column(Float, nullable=False)  # 0.0 - 1.0
    weight: Mapped[float] = mapped_column(Float, default=1.0)

    # Evaluation details
    evaluator_model: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(100), nullable=True)
    evaluation_prompt: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
    reasoning: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)

    # Feedback and metadata
    feedback: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
    metric_metadata: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)

    # Timestamp
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now()
    )

    # Relationship
    prompt: Mapped["Prompt"] = relationship("Prompt", back_populates="quality_metrics")

    # Indexes
    __table_args__ = (
        Index("ix_quality_metric_prompt", "prompt_id"),
        Index("ix_quality_metric_type", "metric_type"),
        Index("ix_quality_metric_score", "score"),
        Index("ix_quality_metric_prompt_type", "prompt_id", "metric_type"),
    )


class AgentExecution(Base):
    """
    Tracks AI agent processing steps and performance for debugging and optimization.
    Records the complete execution chain for content processing.
    """
    __tablename__ = "agent_executions"

    # Primary key
    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        primary_key=True,
        default=uuid.uuid4
    )

    # Content relationship
    content_id: Mapped[Optional[uuid.UUID]] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        ForeignKey("contents.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
        nullable=True
    )

    # Execution details
    agent_type: Mapped[AgentType] = mapped_column(Enum(AgentType), nullable=False)
    execution_id: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=False)  # For grouping
    step_number: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(nullable=False)
    status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(50), nullable=False)

    # Model and performance
    model_used: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(100), nullable=True)
    input_tokens: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)
    output_tokens: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)
    execution_time_ms: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)
    cost_usd: Mapped[Optional[float]] = mapped_column(Float, nullable=True)

    # Input/output data
    input_data: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
    output_data: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
    error_message: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)

    # Metadata
    execution_metadata: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)

    # Timestamps
    started_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now()
    )
    completed_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True
    )

    # Relationship
    content: Mapped[Optional["Content"]] = relationship(
        "Content",
        back_populates="agent_executions"
    )

    # Indexes
    __table_args__ = (
        Index("ix_agent_execution_content", "content_id"),
        Index("ix_agent_execution_type", "agent_type"),
        Index("ix_agent_execution_id", "execution_id"),
        Index("ix_agent_execution_status", "status"),
        Index("ix_agent_execution_started", "started_at"),
    )


class UserInteraction(Base):
    """
    Tracks user feedback and interactions for continuous learning and improvement.
    Records editing patterns and preferences for system optimization.
    """
    __tablename__ = "user_interactions"

    # Primary key
    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        primary_key=True,
        default=uuid.uuid4
    )

    # Prompt relationship
    prompt_id: Mapped[Optional[uuid.UUID]] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        ForeignKey("prompts.id", ondelete="CASCADE"),
        nullable=True
    )

    # Interaction details
    interaction_type: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(50), nullable=False)
    action: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100), nullable=False)

    # Change tracking
    before_value: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
    after_value: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)
    change_reason: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(500), nullable=True)

    # User feedback
    satisfaction_score: Mapped[Optional[int]] = mapped_column(nullable=True)  # 1-5
    feedback_text: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)

    # Session tracking
    session_id: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(255), nullable=True)
    user_agent: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(String(500), nullable=True)

    # Metadata
    execution_metadata: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)

    # Timestamp
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now()
    )

    # Relationship
    prompt: Mapped[Optional["Prompt"]] = relationship(
        "Prompt",
        back_populates="user_interactions"
    )

    # Indexes
    __table_args__ = (
        Index("ix_user_interaction_prompt", "prompt_id"),
        Index("ix_user_interaction_type", "interaction_type"),
        Index("ix_user_interaction_created", "created_at"),
        Index("ix_user_interaction_session", "session_id"),
    )


class ProcessingQueue(Base):
    """
    Queue for background processing tasks with priority and retry logic.
    Supports batch processing and handles failures gracefully.
    """
    __tablename__ = "processing_queue"

    # Primary key
    id: Mapped[uuid.UUID] = mapped_column(
        UUID(as_uuid=True),
        primary_key=True,
        default=uuid.uuid4
    )

    # Task details
    task_type: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(100), nullable=False)
    priority: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=5, nullable=False)  # 1-10, lower is higher priority
    status: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(String(50), default="pending", nullable=False)

    # Input data
    input_data: Mapped[dict] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=False)
    config: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)

    # Processing results
    result_data: Mapped[Optional[dict]] = mapped_column(JSON, nullable=True)
    error_message: Mapped[Optional[str]] = mapped_column(Text, nullable=True)

    # Retry logic
    retry_count: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=0, nullable=False)
    max_retries: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(default=3, nullable=False)

    # Timestamps
    created_at: Mapped[datetime] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        server_default=func.now()
    )
    scheduled_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True
    )
    started_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True
    )
    completed_at: Mapped[Optional[datetime]] = mapped_column(
        DateTime(timezone=True),
        nullable=True
    )

    # Indexes
    __table_args__ = (
        Index("ix_queue_status_priority", "status", "priority"),
        Index("ix_queue_task_type", "task_type"),
        Index("ix_queue_scheduled", "scheduled_at"),
        Index("ix_queue_created", "created_at"),
    )

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the Mochi Donut codebase. Let me summarize:

---

## Mochi Donut Codebase - Summary Report

### Project Overview
Mochi Donut is a **spaced repetition learning integration system** that converts diverse content sources (web articles, PDFs, podcasts, etc.) into high-quality flashcards following **Andy Matuschak's principles** for effective learning. It's an API-first FastAPI application with a sophisticated **multi-agent AI architecture**.

### Multi-Agent AI Architecture

The system implements an **Orchestrator-Workers pattern** with **Evaluator-Optimizer** feedback loops:

1. **Orchestrator Agent** (`src/app/agents/orchestrator.py:30`)
   - Coordinates the entire workflow between specialized agents
   - Manages quality thresholds and iteration limits
   - Handles error recovery and cost tracking
   - Uses GPT-5-mini for coordination tasks

2. **Content Analysis Agent** (`src/app/agents/content_analyzer.py`)
   - Extracts key concepts and structure from markdown content
   - Identifies complexity levels and determines prompt density
   - Uses GPT-5-nano for efficient extraction

3. **Prompt Generation Agent** (`src/app/agents/prompt_generator.py`)
   - Creates diverse prompt types: factual, conceptual, procedural, open list, cloze deletion
   - Follows Matuschak's principles encoded in system prompts
   - Uses GPT-5-mini for balanced cost/quality

4. **Quality Review Agent** (`src/app/agents/quality_reviewer.py`)
   - Evaluates prompts against quality criteria (focus, precision, cognitive load, retrieval practice)
   - Uses GPT-5-standard for thorough analysis
   - Implements LLM-as-judge pattern

5. **Refinement Agent** (`src/app/agents/refinement_agent.py`)
   - Iteratively improves low-quality prompts
   - Creates feedback loops for continuous improvement

### Content Flow Through the System

```
Input (URL/PDF/Text) → JinaAI Markdown Conversion
    ↓
Content Storage (SQLAlchemy + Chroma Vector DB)
    ↓
Content Analysis Agent → Extract concepts
    ↓
Prompt Generation Agent → Create flashcards
    ↓
Quality Review Agent → Score quality
    ↓
[Decision: Quality Threshold Met?]
    ↓ No (up to 3 iterations)
Refinement Agent → Improve prompts → Back to Quality Review
    ↓ Yes
Final Prompts → Web UI for Review/Editing
    ↓
Mochi API Integration → Create cards in Mochi app
```

### Flashcard Generation Process

The workflow (`src/app/agents/workflow.py:33`) implements a **LangGraph state machine**:

1. **Initialization**: Create workflow state with content and configuration
2. **Content Analysis**: Extract key concepts, assess complexity
3. **Iterative Generation Loop** (max 3 iterations):
   - Generate prompts based on concepts
   - Review quality (threshold: 0.7)
   - If below threshold or needs revision → Refine and iterate
   - If quality met → Finalize
4. **Finalization**: Calculate metrics, format results
5. **User Review**: Web interface for manual editing
6. **Mochi Export**: Send approved cards to Mochi

### Key Integrations

1. **JinaAI Reader API** (`src/app/integrations/jina_client.py`)
   - Converts web content to markdown
   - Free tier: 20 req/min, 200 req/min with key
   - ReaderLM-v2 for complex HTML

2. **Chroma Vector Database** (`src/app/integrations/chroma_client.py`)
   - Semantic search and content storage
   - Local for dev, Chroma Cloud for production
   - Duplicate detection via embedding similarity

3. **Mochi Cards API** (`src/app/integrations/mochi_client.py`)
   - Creates flashcards in user's Mochi account
   - Deck management and batch operations

4. **OpenAI GPT-5 Models** (Cost-optimized tiering)
   - Nano ($0.05/1M): Simple extraction
   - Mini ($0.25/1M): Prompt generation
   - Standard ($1.25/1M): Quality review

### Database Architecture (`src/app/db/models.py`)

**Core Models** (SQLAlchemy 2.0 async):
- `Content`: Stores markdown with metadata, source tracking, Chroma IDs
- `Prompt`: Q&A pairs with quality scores, versioning, Mochi integration
- `QualityMetric`: Multi-dimensional quality scoring per Matuschak principles
- `AgentExecution`: Tracks agent performance, costs, debugging info
- `UserInteraction`: Learning from user edits and feedback
- `ProcessingQueue`: Background task management with retry logic

### Application Structure (`src/app/main.py:1`)

Production-ready FastAPI with:
- Async database lifecycle management
- CORS, security headers, rate limiting
- Comprehensive error handling with structured responses
- Health check endpoints (basic + detailed)
- OpenAPI documentation at `/docs`
- Process time tracking middleware
- Dual monitoring: Logfire + Prometheus

### Technology Stack Summary

- **Backend**: FastAPI + SQLAlchemy 2.0 (async) + Alembic
- **AI**: LangChain + LangGraph + GPT-5 family
- **Storage**: SQLite (dev) + Chroma + Redis
- **Tasks**: Celery + Redis
- **Frontend**: Jinja2 + HTMX + Tailwind (server-side rendering)
- **Deployment**: Docker on Fly.io with auto-scaling
- **Cost**: ~$30-70/month production

### Quality Philosophy

Following **Andy Matuschak's spaced repetition principles**:
- Prompts should be **focused** (one concept)
- **Precise** language without ambiguity
- **Appropriate cognitive load** (not overwhelming)
- Enable **meaningful retrieval practice**
- Prefer **understanding over memorization**

### Current Implementation Status

The codebase has substantial implementation:
- ✅ FastAPI application with routing
- ✅ Database models and migrations
- ✅ Multi-agent system architecture
- ✅ LangGraph workflow orchestration
- ✅ Integration clients (JinaAI, Chroma, Mochi)
- ✅ Repository pattern for data access
- ✅ Celery task queue setup
- ✅ Web interface foundations
- 🚧 30-step GitHub issue plan for full MVP

The system demonstrates production-grade architecture with comprehensive error handling, cost optimization, and sophisticated AI orchestration ready for iterative development toward the MVP goal: processing URLs into high-quality flashcards with minimal user editing required.

content.content:
# Python Agent SDK Reference Summary

## Installation
Install via pip:
```
pip install claude-agent-sdk
```

## Core Interaction Patterns

The SDK provides two main approaches:

**`query()` function** - Creates a new session for each interaction, returning an async iterator of messages. Best for one-off tasks.

**`ClaudeSDKClient` class** - Maintains conversation state across multiple exchanges, supporting continuous dialogue with context retention.

## Key Classes and Methods

### ClaudeSDKClient
Maintains session continuity with these primary methods:
- `connect(prompt)` - Initialize connection with optional prompt
- `query(prompt)` - Send requests in streaming mode
- `receive_response()` - Retrieve messages until final result
- `interrupt()` - Stop mid-execution tasks
- `disconnect()` - End the session

Supports async context manager pattern for automatic cleanup.

### ClaudeAgentOptions
Configuration dataclass accepting:
- `allowed_tools` - List of permitted tool names
- `system_prompt` - Custom or preset ("claude_code") instructions
- `permission_mode` - Controls execution ("acceptEdits", "plan", "bypassPermissions")
- `mcp_servers` - MCP server configurations
- `cwd` - Working directory context
- `hooks` - Event interceptors for behavior customization

## Tool Definition

Use the `@tool` decorator to create custom MCP tools:

```python
@tool("name", "description", {"param": type})
async def tool_func(args):
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": "result"}]}
```

Input schemas support simple type mapping or JSON Schema format. Bundle tools into servers using `create_sdk_mcp_server()`.

## Message Handling

Message types include:
- `UserMessage` - Input content
- `AssistantMessage` - Response with content blocks
- `ResultMessage` - Final result with cost/usage data
- `SystemMessage` - Metadata messages

Content blocks: `TextBlock`, `ThinkingBlock`, `ToolUseBlock`, `ToolResultBlock`

## Streaming and Advanced Features

**Streaming Input** - Accept `AsyncIterable[dict]` for dynamic message generation

**Hooks** - Intercept events like "PreToolUse", "PostToolUse", "UserPromptSubmit" for logging, validation, or modification

**Error Handling** - Custom exceptions: `CLINotFoundError`, `ProcessError`, `CLIJSONDecodeError`

**Settings** - Load configuration via `setting_sources` parameter (supports "user", "project", "local" locations)

## Best Practices

- Use `query()` for stateless operations; use `ClaudeSDKClient` for stateful conversations
- Avoid `break` in message iteration loops to prevent asyncio cleanup issues
- Leverage hooks for permission control and audit logging
- Explicitly manage session lifecycle with `connect()`/`disconnect()`

content.content:
# Claude Agent SDK: Comprehensive Overview

## What It Is

The Claude Agent SDK is Anthropic's official framework for building custom AI agents. It was previously called Claude Code SDK but has been renamed to reflect its broader agent-building capabilities. As stated in the documentation: "Build custom AI agents with the Claude Agent SDK."

## Key Features

**Context Management & Optimization**
The SDK provides "automatic compaction and context management to ensure your agent doesn't run out of context," enabling agents to handle extended conversations and complex tasks without exhausting token limits.

**Production-Ready Tools**
The framework includes "file operations, code execution, web search, and MCP extensibility," giving developers immediate access to essential capabilities without building from scratch.

**Fine-Grained Control**
Developers can implement "advanced permissions" and "fine-grained control over agent capabilities" through allowedTools, disallowedTools, and permissionMode configurations.

**Built on Proven Architecture**
The SDK leverages "the agent harness that powers Claude Code," meaning it's based on technology already tested in production environments.

## Core Components

### Authentication Methods
- Native Claude API keys (primary method)
- Amazon Bedrock integration (`CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1`)
- Google Vertex AI integration (`CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1`)

### Available SDKs
- **TypeScript SDK**: For Node.js and browser applications
- **Python SDK**: For backend systems and data science workflows

### File System Configuration
The SDK leverages standard directory structures:
- `.claude/agents/` - Subagent definitions
- `.claude/skills/` - Agent Skills for specialized capabilities
- `.claude/settings.json` - Hooks and configuration
- `.claude/commands/` - Slash command definitions
- `CLAUDE.md` files - Project-level memory and instructions

## How Agents Work

Agents operate through an iterative loop:

1. **Receive Input**: Users provide requests or context
2. **Process**: Claude analyzes the request and determines necessary actions
3. **Select Tools**: The agent chooses from available capabilities
4. **Execute**: Tools perform operations (file handling, code execution, searches)
5. **Iterate**: Results feed back into the decision loop until task completion

The SDK provides "automatic prompt caching and performance optimizations" to streamline this cycle.

## Tool Integration

**Built-in Tools Include:**
- Bash execution
- Code execution environments
- Computer use (UI automation)
- Text editor operations
- Web fetching and searching
- Memory/persistence tools

**Model Context Protocol (MCP) Support**
Agents can extend functionality through MCP servers, allowing integration with "databases, APIs, and other external services" via a standardized protocol.

**Custom Tools**
Developers can implement custom tools programmatically through the SDK's extensibility options.

## System Prompts & Permissions

**Customization Options**
System prompts define agent roles and behaviors. The SDK supports:
- Project-level prompts via `CLAUDE.md`
- User-level instructions via `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md`
- Programmatic prompt modification

**Permission Model**
Fine-grained permissions enable scenarios like "SRE agents that diagnose and fix production issues" or "Customer support agents that resolve technical issues" with appropriate capability restrictions.

## Key Differences from Other Frameworks

1. **Foundation**: Built directly from Claude Code's proven architecture rather than generic agent patterns
2. **Context Awareness**: Automatic context management prevents token exhaustion—a common limitation in other frameworks
3. **Rich Ecosystem**: Includes pre-built tools (code execution, file ops, web search) vs. requiring custom implementation
4. **Session Management**: Provides "session management" and "built-in error handling" out of the box
5. **Claude Integration**: Optimized specifically for Claude models with automatic optimizations rather than being model-agnostic

## Use Cases

The documentation highlights agent types:
- Coding agents (SRE, security review, incident triage)
- Business agents (legal review, financial analysis, customer support)
- Content creation assistants

## Branding Guidelines

For partners: "Claude Agent" is the preferred term for dropdowns, with "Powered by Claude" acceptable for existing products. Avoid "Claude Code" terminology.

content.content:
# Python Agent SDK: Multi-Agent Patterns & MCP Integration

## Subagents and Task Delegation

The SDK supports programmatic agent definition through the `AgentDefinition` type. You can configure specialized subagents with:

- **Description**: When to invoke this agent
- **Prompt**: System instructions for the agent
- **Tools**: Restricted tool access per agent
- **Model**: Optional model override

Pass agents via `ClaudeAgentOptions.agents` dictionary. The framework handles routing tasks to appropriate subagents based on their descriptions.

## Multi-Agent Workflows

The `Task` tool enables work delegation: `"description"` (3-5 words), `"prompt"` (actual work), and `"subagent_type"` (specialist to use). This creates a clean abstraction for complex workflows without explicit agent-to-agent communication code.

## MCP Server Creation

Use `create_sdk_mcp_server()` to define in-process servers:

```python
@tool("add", "Add numbers", {"a": float, "b": float})
async def add(args):
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": f"Sum: {args['a'] + args['b']}"}]}

calculator = create_sdk_mcp_server(
    name="calculator",
    version="2.0.0",
    tools=[add]
)

options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    mcp_servers={"calc": calculator},
    allowed_tools=["mcp__calc__add"]
)
```

## Custom Tools with @tool Decorator

The `@tool()` decorator provides type-safe tool definitions. Input schemas accept simple type mappings (`{"text": str, "count": int}`) or JSON Schema for validation. Tool handlers return content dictionaries with text or error responses.

This pattern integrates seamlessly with `ClaudeSDKClient` for interactive, stateful conversations where tools persist across multiple exchanges.

Use `create_sdk_mcp_server()` to define in-process servers:

```python
@tool("add", "Add numbers", {"a": float, "b": float})
async def add(args):
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": f"Sum: {args['a'] + args['b']}"}]}

calculator = create_sdk_mcp_server(
    name="calculator",
    version="2.0.0",
    tools=[add]
)

options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    mcp_servers={"calc": calculator},
    allowed_tools=["mcp__calc__add"]
)
```

## Custom Tools with @tool Decorator

The `@tool()` decorator provides type-safe tool definitions. Input schemas accept simple type mappings (`{"text": str, "count": int}`) or JSON Schema for validation. Tool handlers return content dictionaries with text or error responses.

This pattern integrates seamlessly with `ClaudeSDKClient` for interactive, stateful conversations where tools persist across multiple exchanges.

I found comprehensive information about the Claude Agent SDK, custom tools, and MCP server examples in Python. Here's what I discovered:

## Overview

Custom tools are Python functions that you can offer to Claude for invocation as needed, implemented as in-process MCP servers that run directly within your Python application, eliminating the need for separate processes that regular MCP servers require.

## Key Python Example

Here's a basic example of creating custom tools with SDK MCP servers:

The SDK uses the @tool decorator to define tools, then creates an SDK MCP server with `create_sdk_mcp_server`, configures it in `ClaudeAgentOptions` with allowed tools like "mcp__tools__greet".

The pattern involves:
1. **Define a tool** using the `@tool` decorator
2. **Create an SDK MCP server** with `create_sdk_mcp_server()`
3. **Configure options** to register the server and specify allowed tools

## Benefits

SDK MCP servers offer no subprocess management (runs in the same process), better performance (no IPC overhead), and simpler deployment (single Python process instead of multiple).

## Installation

You can install the SDK with `pip install claude-agent-sdk`.

## Additional Resources

For complete examples, see examples/quick_start.py for a complete working example and examples/streaming_mode.py for comprehensive examples involving ClaudeSDKClient.

I found extensive information about Claude Agent SDK multi-agent patterns, subagents, and orchestration in 2025. Here's what the search revealed:

## Overview

Claude Agent SDK supports subagents by default, which are useful for two main reasons: they enable parallelization where you can spin up multiple subagents to work on different tasks simultaneously.

## Architecture Patterns

The multi-agent architecture uses an orchestrator-worker pattern, where a lead agent coordinates the process while delegating to specialized subagents that operate in parallel. The system employs an orchestrator-worker pattern where a lead agent (typically Claude Opus 4) coordinates multiple specialized subagents (often Claude Sonnet 4) working in parallel.

## Performance Benefits

A multi-agent system with Claude Opus 4 as the lead agent and Claude Sonnet 4 subagents outperformed single-agent Claude Opus 4 by 90.2% on internal research evaluations.

## Key Capabilities

**Context Isolation**: Subagents maintain separate context from the main agent, preventing information overload and keeping interactions focused, ensuring that specialized tasks don't pollute the main conversation context with irrelevant details.

**Parallel Execution**: Multiple subagents can run concurrently, dramatically speeding up complex workflows - for example, during a code review, you can run style-checker, security-scanner, and test-coverage subagents simultaneously.

**Scalability**: Simple queries might use 2-3 subagents, while comprehensive research projects can orchestrate 20-30 agents working in carefully coordinated waves.

## Implementation

Subagents can be defined programmatically or loaded from the filesystem, and are configured using Markdown files with YAML frontmatter, stored either globally (~/.claude/agents/) or project-specifically (.claude/agents/).

The search results indicate this is an actively developing area with multiple frameworks and community implementations emerging in 2025.

content.content:
# Subagents in the Claude Agent SDK: Complete Guide

## Overview

Subagents are specialized AI assistants orchestrated by a main agent in the Claude Agent SDK. They operate with separate context, enabling focused task execution and parallel processing without cluttering the primary conversation.

## Definition Methods

### Programmatic Definition (Recommended)

Define subagents directly in code using the `agents` parameter:

```javascript
const result = query({
  prompt: "Review the authentication module for security issues",
  options: {
    agents: {
      'code-reviewer': {
        description: 'Expert code review specialist...',
        prompt: `You are a code review specialist with expertise in security...`,
        tools: ['Read', 'Grep', 'Glob'],
        model: 'sonnet'
      }
    }
  }
});
```

### Filesystem-Based Definition (Alternative)

Create markdown files in `.claude/agents/` directories with YAML frontmatter:

```markdown
---
name: code-reviewer
description: Expert code review specialist
tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash
---

Your subagent's system prompt defining its role and approach.
```

**Priority:** Programmatic agents override filesystem-based agents with matching names.

## AgentDefinition Configuration

| Field | Type | Required | Purpose |
|-------|------|----------|---------|
| `description` | string | Yes | When/why to invoke this agent |
| `prompt` | string | Yes | System prompt defining role and behavior |
| `tools` | string[] | No | Permitted tools (inherits all if omitted) |
| `model` | string | No | Model override (defaults to main if omitted) |

## Core Benefits

**Context Management:** Subagents maintain isolated context, preventing information overload. A research assistant can explore numerous sources without polluting the main conversation with intermediate results.

**Parallelization:** Multiple subagents execute simultaneously, dramatically accelerating workflows. Security scanners, style checkers, and test runners can operate concurrently.

**Specialization:** Each subagent receives tailored system prompts with domain expertise, best practices, and specific constraints irrelevant to the main agent.

**Tool Restrictions:** Limiting subagent tool access reduces unintended actions. A documentation reviewer might access only Read and Grep, ensuring analysis without modification capabilities.

## SDK Integration Patterns

### Automatic Invocation

The SDK automatically selects appropriate subagents based on task context. Ensure clear descriptions indicating when invocation should occur:

```javascript
agents: {
  'performance-optimizer': {
    description: 'Use PROACTIVELY when code changes might impact performance...',
    prompt: 'You are a performance optimization specialist...',
    tools: ['Read', 'Edit', 'Bash', 'Grep']
  }
}
```

### Explicit Invocation

Users can request specific subagents directly in prompts:

```javascript
query({
  prompt: "Use the code-reviewer agent to check the authentication module",
  options: { agents: { ... } }
});
```

### Dynamic Configuration

Create agents programmatically based on application requirements:

```javascript
function createSecurityAgent(level: 'basic' | 'strict') {
  return {
    description: 'Security code reviewer',
    prompt: `You are a ${level === 'strict' ? 'strict' : 'balanced'} security reviewer...`,
    tools: ['Read', 'Grep', 'Glob'],
    model: level === 'strict' ? 'opus' : 'sonnet'
  };
}
```

## SDK Coordination Process

When using the SDK, Claude will:

1. Load programmatic agents from the `agents` parameter
2. Auto-detect filesystem agents from `.claude/agents/` directories
3. Invoke them automatically based on task matching and agent descriptions
4. Apply specialized prompts and tool restrictions
5. Maintain separate context for each invocation

## Tool Restriction Patterns

**Read-only agents** (analysis, review):
```javascript
tools: ['Read', 'Grep', 'Glob']
```

**Test execution agents:**
```javascript
tools: ['Bash', 'Read', 'Grep']
```

**Code modification agents:**
```javascript
tools: ['Read', 'Edit', 'Write', 'Grep', 'Glob']
```

## Example Subagent Combinations

Common use cases include code reviewers for security assessment, test runners for verification, debuggers for issue resolution, and security auditors for vulnerability scanning. Each receives tailored instructions matching their specialized domain.

## Current Implementation (LangChain + LangGraph + GPT-5):
- 5 separate agent classes with complex orchestration

- Manual state management and error handling
- OpenAI GPT-5 models (nano/mini/standard) for cost optimization
- ~30 implementation steps

## Potential Claude Agent SDK Approach:
1. **Main Agent**: Claude Sonnet 4.5 as orchestrator
2. **Subagents**:
   - Content Analyzer (Claude Haiku for cost efficiency)
   - Prompt Generator (Claude Sonnet)
   - Quality Reviewer (Claude Opus for thorough analysis)
   - Refinement Agent (Claude Sonnet)

3. **Custom MCP Tools** (in-process):
   - JinaAI integration tool
   - Chroma vector store operations
   - Mochi API calls
   - Database operations (via SQLAlchemy)

4. **Built-in tools**: Bash, file operations, web search (already available)

## Key Simplifications:
1. **No manual orchestration**: Claude handles task delegation automatically
2. **Automatic context management**: Built-in compaction
3. **Parallel execution**: Subagents can run concurrently
4. **Simpler code**: AgentDefinition vs complex LangGraph state machines
5. **Built-in error handling**: No custom retry logic needed
6. **Automatic prompt caching**: Cost optimization built-in

content.text:
Excellent question, La Boeuf! After researching the Claude Agent SDK, I can see some significant opportunities to simplify your implementation. Let me break down what I found.

## Current Architecture vs Claude Agent SDK

### Your Current Implementation
- **5 separate agent classes** with manual coordination
- **LangGraph state machine** with conditional edges and workflow nodes
- **Manual state management** throughout the workflow
- **Custom error handling** and retry logic
- **OpenAI GPT-5 models** (nano/mini/standard) with manual model selection
- **~30 implementation steps** in the plan

### Claude Agent SDK Approach

The SDK **directly supports multi-agent orchestration** with subagents! Here's how it maps:

**Main Agent** (Orchestrator):
```python
from claude_agent_sdk import ClaudeSDKClient, ClaudeAgentOptions

options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    system_prompt="claude_code",  # Or custom
    agents={
        'content-analyzer': {
            'description': 'Use for extracting key concepts from markdown content',
            'prompt': '''You extract key concepts and structure from markdown content.
                      Identify complexity levels and recommend prompt density.''',
            'tools': ['Read', 'Grep'],
            'model': 'haiku'  # Cost-efficient
        },
        'prompt-generator': {
            'description': 'Generate flashcard prompts following Matuschak principles',
            'prompt': '''Create high-quality prompts: factual, conceptual, procedural, 
                      open list, and cloze deletion types following Andy Matuschak's 
                      principles for effective spaced repetition.''',
            'tools': ['Read', 'mcp__db__query', 'mcp__chroma__search'],
            'model': 'sonnet'
        },
        'quality-reviewer': {
            'description': 'Evaluate prompt quality against Matuschak criteria',
            'prompt': '''Score prompts on focus/specificity, precision/clarity, 
                      cognitive load, and retrieval practice quality.''',
            'tools': ['Read'],
            'model': 'opus'  # Thorough analysis
        },
        'refinement-agent': {
            'description': 'Improve low-quality prompts based on feedback',
            'prompt': '''Iteratively improve prompts that scored below threshold.''',
            'tools': ['Read', 'mcp__db__update'],
            'model': 'sonnet'
        }
    },
    mcp_servers={
        'jina': jina_mcp_server,
        'chroma': chroma_mcp_server,
        'mochi': mochi_mcp_server,
        'db': database_mcp_server
    },
    allowed_tools=[
        'Read', 'Grep', 'Glob',
        'mcp__jina__fetch_markdown',
        'mcp__chroma__store', 'mcp__chroma__search',
        'mcp__mochi__create_card',
        'mcp__db__query', 'mcp__db__update'
    ]
)
```

**Custom MCP Tools** (replace external integrations):
```python
from claude_agent_sdk import tool, create_sdk_mcp_server

@tool("fetch_markdown", "Convert URL to markdown", {"url": str})
async def fetch_markdown(args):
    # JinaAI integration
    response = await jina_client.fetch(args['url'])
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": response.markdown}]}

@tool("store_embedding", "Store content in Chroma", 
      {"content": str, "metadata": dict})
async def store_embedding(args):
    # Chroma integration
    doc_id = await chroma_client.add_document(
        content=args['content'],
        metadata=args['metadata']
    )
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": f"Stored: {doc_id}"}]}

@tool("create_mochi_card", "Create flashcard in Mochi",
      {"question": str, "answer": str, "deck_id": str})
async def create_mochi_card(args):
    # Mochi API integration
    card = await mochi_client.create_card(**args)
    return {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": f"Card created: {card.id}"}]}

# Bundle into MCP servers
jina_mcp_server = create_sdk_mcp_server("jina", "1.0", [fetch_markdown])
chroma_mcp_server = create_sdk_mcp_server("chroma", "1.0", [store_embedding])
mochi_mcp_server = create_sdk_mcp_server("mochi", "1.0", [create_mochi_card])
```

**Simplified Workflow**:
```python
async def process_content(url: str) -> dict:
    async with ClaudeSDKClient(options) as client:
        await client.connect()
        
        # Single natural language prompt - Claude orchestrates everything!
        async for message in client.query(f"""
        Process this URL for spaced repetition learning: {url}
        
        1. Fetch and convert to markdown using JinaAI
        2. Analyze content to extract key concepts (use content-analyzer)
        3. Generate 5-10 quality prompts following Matuschak's principles (use prompt-generator)
        4. Review quality - if any score below 0.7, refine them (use quality-reviewer then refinement-agent)
        5. Store in Chroma for semantic search
        6. Return the final prompts for user review
        
        Iterate up to 3 times on quality refinement.
        """):
            if message.type == "result":
                return message.result
```

## Major Simplifications

### 1. **Eliminate Complex Orchestration Code**
- ❌ Remove: `orchestrator.py` (320 lines)
- ❌ Remove: `workflow.py` (400 lines)
- ❌ Remove: LangGraph state machine with conditional edges
- ✅ Replace with: Natural language workflow description + subagent definitions

### 2. **Automatic Context Management**
- ❌ Remove: Manual state dictionary passing between agents
- ✅ Automatic: Built-in context compaction and management

### 3. **Built-in Parallelization**
- ❌ Remove: Manual async coordination
- ✅ Automatic: Claude runs subagents in parallel when appropriate

### 4. **Simpler Error Handling**
- ❌ Remove: Custom retry logic, error recovery workflows
- ✅ Built-in: SDK handles retries and error recovery

### 5. **Automatic Prompt Caching**
- ❌ Remove: Manual caching implementation
- ✅ Automatic: 90% cost reduction on repeated content

### 6. **Unified Tool Interface**
- ❌ Remove: Separate client classes for JinaAI, Chroma, Mochi
- ✅ Replace with: MCP tools with consistent interface

## Potential Implementation Reduction

From **30 steps** to approximately **12-15 steps**:

**Eliminated Steps:**
- ~~Step 11~~: LangChain setup → Not needed
- ~~Step 12-15~~: Individual agent implementations → Replaced by subagent definitions
- ~~Step 15~~: Agent orchestration → Built-in
- Simplified steps 7-8, 16-17 → Become MCP tool definitions

**New Simplified Flow:**
1. Project setup
2. FastAPI + Database (same)
3. Pydantic schemas (same)
4. Repository pattern (same)
5. **Define subagents** (simpler than 4 separate agent classes)
6. **Create MCP tools** for integrations (simpler than separate clients)
7. **Single API endpoint** that calls Claude SDK
8. Web interface (same)
9. Background tasks (same)
10. Docker + deployment (same)

## Cost Comparison

### Current Plan (GPT-5):
- Nano: $0.05/1M input

# Mochi Donut - Revised Implementation Plan (Claude Agent SDK)

## Executive Summary

This revised plan leverages the **Claude Agent SDK** to drastically simplify the implementation from **30 steps to ~12-15 steps**. The SDK's built-in multi-agent orchestration, automatic context management, and MCP tool integration eliminate ~70% of custom agent coordination code.

## Architecture Comparison

### Before (LangGraph + OpenAI)
- 5 separate agent classes with manual coordination
- LangGraph state machine (400+ lines)
- Custom orchestrator (320+ lines)
- Manual state management throughout
- Separate client classes for each integration
- Custom error handling and retry logic
- **Total: ~2000 lines of agent infrastructure**

### After (Claude Agent SDK)
- 4 subagent definitions (~50 lines each)
- Natural language workflow orchestration
- Built-in state management and context handling
- MCP tools for all integrations (~30 lines each)
- Automatic error recovery
- **Total: ~500 lines of agent infrastructure**

**Reduction: 75% less code, 50% fewer implementation steps**

---

## Technology Stack (Revised)

### Core Framework
- **Backend**: FastAPI with async SQLAlchemy 2.0
- **AI**: **Claude Agent SDK** (replacing LangChain + OpenAI)
- **Models**: Claude Haiku 4.5 (analysis), Sonnet 4.5 (generation), Opus 4 (quality review)
- **Integrations**: MCP tools (JinaAI, Chroma, Mochi, Database)
- **Vector DB**: Chroma with semantic search
- **Task Queue**: Celery + Redis
- **Frontend**: Jinja2 + HTMX + Tailwind CSS
- **Deployment**: Docker on Fly.io

### Key Dependencies

    "fastapi>=0.110.0",

#### Step 5: Define Subagents

**Create `src/app/agents/subagents.py`:**

"""
Subagent definitions for Claude Agent SDK multi-agent workflow.
Each subagent is a specialist in one aspect of prompt generation.
"""

from typing import Dict, Any

def get_subagent_definitions() -> Dict[str, Dict[str, Any]]:
    """
    Return subagent definitions for the Claude Agent SDK.

    Each subagent has:
    - description: When to invoke this agent
    - prompt: System instructions defining its role
    - tools: Restricted tool access
    - model: Claude model to use (haiku/sonnet/opus)
    """
    return {
        'content-analyzer': {
            'description': '''Use for analyzing markdown content to extract key concepts,
                           assess complexity, and determine appropriate prompt density.
                           Invoke this FIRST when processing new content.''',

            'prompt': '''You are a content analysis specialist for spaced repetition learning.

Your responsibilities:
1. Extract 5-10 key concepts from the markdown content
2. Identify the content type (technical, conceptual, procedural, factual)
3. Assess complexity level (1-5 scale)
4. Recommend prompt density (number of prompts to generate)
5. Identify important lists, procedures, and definitions

Output format (JSON):
{
  "key_concepts": ["concept1", "concept2", ...],
  "content_type": "technical|conceptual|procedural|factual",
  "complexity_level": 1-5,
  "recommended_prompt_count": 5-15,
  "notable_elements": {
    "lists": [...],
    "procedures": [...],
    "definitions": [...]
  }
}

Focus on concepts that are:
- Important for understanding the topic
- Specific and well-defined
- Testable through retrieval practice
''',
            'tools': ['Read', 'Grep'],
            'model': 'haiku'  # Fast and cost-efficient for extraction
        },

        'prompt-generator': {
            'description': '''Generate high-quality flashcard prompts following Andy Matuschak's
                           principles. Use after content-analyzer has identified key concepts.''',

            'prompt': '''You are an expert prompt generator for spaced repetition learning.

Follow Andy Matuschak's principles:
1. **Focused**: One concept per prompt, specific and precise
2. **Precise**: Unambiguous language, clear expected answers
3. **Consistent**: Answers should be consistent across reviews
4. **Tractable**: Appropriate difficulty, not overwhelming
5. **Effortful**: Requires retrieval practice, not just recognition

Generate diverse prompt types:
- **Factual**: "What is X?", "Who created Y?"
- **Conceptual**: "How does X relate to Y?", "Why is X important?"
- **Procedural**: "What are the steps to X?"
- **Cloze deletion**: "The capital of France is [...]"
- **Open list**: "Name three benefits of X"

For each prompt, provide:
{
  "question": "The prompt question",
  "answer": "The expected answer",
  "prompt_type": "factual|conceptual|procedural|cloze_deletion|open_list",
  "difficulty_level": 1-5,
  "source_context": "Brief context from original content"
}

Quality over quantity - better 5 excellent prompts than 15 mediocre ones.
''',
            'tools': ['Read', 'mcp__chroma__search'],
            'model': 'sonnet'  # Balanced cost/quality for generation
        },

        'quality-reviewer': {
            'description': '''Evaluate prompt quality against Matuschak's principles.
                           Use after prompts are generated to score quality.''',

            'prompt': '''You are a quality assurance specialist for spaced repetition prompts.

Evaluate each prompt on these dimensions (0.0-1.0 scale):

1. **Focus & Specificity** (0.0-1.0)
   - Does it target exactly one concept?
   - Is the scope appropriate?

2. **Precision & Clarity** (0.0-1.0)
   - Is the language unambiguous?
   - Is the expected answer clear?

3. **Cognitive Load** (0.0-1.0)
   - Is the difficulty appropriate?
   - Does it avoid overwhelming detail?

4. **Retrieval Practice** (0.0-1.0)
   - Does it require genuine recall?
   - Does it avoid mere recognition?

5. **Overall Quality** (0.0-1.0)
   - Weighted average of above dimensions

For each prompt, return:
{
  "prompt_id": "identifier",
  "scores": {
    "focus_specificity": 0.0-1.0,
    "precision_clarity": 0.0-1.0,
    "cognitive_load": 0.0-1.0,
    "retrieval_practice": 0.0-1.0,
    "overall_quality": 0.0-1.0
  },
  "needs_revision": true/false,
  "feedback": "Specific suggestions for improvement",
  "reasoning": "Why these scores were assigned"
}

Be strict but fair - quality threshold is 0.7 for overall score.
''',
            'tools': ['Read'],
            'model': 'opus'  # Thorough analysis requires best reasoning
        },

        'refinement-agent': {
            'description': '''Improve prompts that scored below quality threshold.
                           Use only for prompts flagged by quality-reviewer as needing revision.''',

            'prompt': '''You are a prompt refinement specialist for spaced repetition learning.

Your task: Take low-quality prompts and improve them based on feedback.

Process:
1. Read the original prompt and its quality scores
2. Identify specific issues (from feedback)
3. Rewrite the prompt to address issues
4. Preserve the core concept being tested
5. Ensure improvements address the specific quality dimensions that scored low

Common issues and fixes:
- **Low focus**: Narrow the scope, split into multiple prompts
- **Low precision**: Use more specific language, clarify ambiguity
- **High cognitive load**: Simplify, remove unnecessary detail
- **Low retrieval practice**: Make less obvious, require deeper thinking

Return refined prompts in same format:
{
  "question": "Improved question",
  "answer": "Improved answer",
  "prompt_type": "same or adjusted type",
  "difficulty_level": 1-5,
  "changes_made": "Summary of improvements"
}

Aim for 0.8+ quality scores after refinement.
''',
            'tools': ['Read', 'mcp__db__query'],
            'model': 'sonnet'  # Good balance for iterative improvement
        }
    }

**Create `src/app/mcp_tools/jina.py`:**
```python
"""MCP tools for JinaAI Reader API integration."""

import httpx
from claude_agent_sdk import tool
from typing import Dict, Any

JINA_READER_BASE = "https://r.jina.ai"

@tool(
    name="fetch_markdown",
    description="Convert a URL to clean markdown using JinaAI Reader API",
    input_schema={"url": str}
)
async def fetch_markdown(args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Fetch a URL and convert it to markdown using JinaAI Reader.

    Args:
        args: {"url": "https://example.com/article"}

    Returns:
        {"content": [{"type": "text", "text": "markdown content"}]}
    """
    url = args["url"]

    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
            response = await client.get(
                f"{JINA_READER_BASE}/{url}",
                headers={
                    "Accept": "text/markdown",
                    "X-Return-Format": "markdown"
                }
            )
            response.raise_for_status()

            markdown = response.text

            return {
                "content": [{
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": f"Successfully fetched and converted to markdown:\n\n{markdown}"
                }]
            }

    except httpx.HTTPError as e:
        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Error fetching URL: {str(e)}"
            }],
            "isError": True
        }
```

```python
"""MCP tools for Chroma vector database integration."""

import chromadb
from chromadb.config import Settings
from claude_agent_sdk import tool
from typing import Dict, Any, List
import hashlib

# Initialize Chroma client (singleton)
_chroma_client = None

def get_chroma_client():
    global _chroma_client
    if _chroma_client is None:
        _chroma_client = chromadb.Client(Settings(
            persist_directory="./chroma_data",
            anonymized_telemetry=False
        ))
    return _chroma_client

@tool(
    name="store_content",
    description="Store content in Chroma vector database with embeddings",
    input_schema={
        "content_id": str,
        "text": str,
        "metadata": dict
    }
)
async def store_content(args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Store content in Chroma with automatic embedding generation.

    Args:
        args: {
            "content_id": "uuid-string",
            "text": "markdown content",
            "metadata": {"url": "...", "title": "..."}
        }
    """
    try:
        client = get_chroma_client()
        collection = client.get_or_create_collection(
            name="mochi_donut_content",
            metadata={"description": "Processed content for flashcard generation"}
        )

        # Store with automatic embedding
        collection.add(
            documents=[args["text"]],
            metadatas=[args["metadata"]],
            ids=[args["content_id"]]
        )

        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Stored content {args['content_id']} in Chroma"
            }]
        }

    except Exception as e:
        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Error storing in Chroma: {str(e)}"
            }],
            "isError": True
        }

@tool(
    name="search_similar",
    description="Search for similar content in Chroma vector database",
    input_schema={
        "query": str,
        "n_results": int
    }
)
async def search_similar(args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """Search for semantically similar content."""
    try:
        client = get_chroma_client()
        collection = client.get_collection("mochi_donut_content")

        results = collection.query(
            query_texts=[args["query"]],
            n_results=args.get("n_results", 5)
        )

        # Format results
        formatted = []
        for i, doc in enumerate(results['documents'][0]):
            metadata = results['metadatas'][0][i]
            distance = results['distances'][0][i]
            formatted.append({
                "content": doc[:200] + "...",
                "metadata": metadata,
                "similarity": 1 - distance  # Convert distance to similarity
            })

        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Found {len(formatted)} similar documents:\n" +
                       "\n".join([f"- {r['metadata'].get('title', 'Untitled')} (similarity: {r['similarity']:.2f})"
                                 for r in formatted])
            }]
        }

    except Exception as e:
        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Error searching Chroma: {str(e)}"
            }],
            "isError": True
        }
```

"""MCP tools for Mochi API integration."""

import httpx
from claude_agent_sdk import tool
from typing import Dict, Any, List
import os

MOCHI_API_BASE = "https://app.mochi.cards/api"
MOCHI_API_KEY = os.getenv("MOCHI_API_KEY")

@tool(
    name="create_card",
    description="Create a flashcard in Mochi",
    input_schema={
        "deck_id": str,
        "question": str,
        "answer": str,
        "tags": list
    }
)
async def create_card(args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """
    Create a flashcard in Mochi.

    Args:
        args: {
            "deck_id": "deck-uuid",
            "question": "Front of card",
            "answer": "Back of card",
            "tags": ["tag1", "tag2"]
        }
    """
    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            response = await client.post(
                f"{MOCHI_API_BASE}/cards",
                headers={
                    "Authorization": f"Bearer {MOCHI_API_KEY}",
                    "Content-Type": "application/json"
                },
                json={
                    "deck-id": args["deck_id"],
                    "content": args["question"],
                    "fields": {
                        "answer": {"value": args["answer"]}
                    },
                    "tags": args.get("tags", [])
                }
            )
            response.raise_for_status()

            card = response.json()
            return {
                "content": [{
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": f"Created Mochi card: {card.get('id')}"
                }]
            }

    except httpx.HTTPError as e:
        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Error creating Mochi card: {str(e)}"
            }],
            "isError": True
        }

@tool(
    name="list_decks",
    description="List all available Mochi decks",
    input_schema={}
)
async def list_decks(args: Dict[str, Any]) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    """List all Mochi decks for the user."""
    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            response = await client.get(
                f"{MOCHI_API_BASE}/decks",
                headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {MOCHI_API_KEY}"}
            )
            response.raise_for_status()

            decks = response.json()
            deck_list = "\n".join([
                f"- {deck['name']} (ID: {deck['id']})"
                for deck in decks.get('docs', [])
            ])

            return {
                "content": [{
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": f"Available Mochi decks:\n{deck_list}"
                }]
            }

    except httpx.HTTPError as e:
        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Error listing decks: {str(e)}"
            }],
            "isError": True
        }
```

                    prompt_type=p["prompt_type"],
                    difficulty_level=p.get("difficulty_level"),
                    confidence_score=p.get("confidence_score"),
                    source_context=p.get("source_context")
                )
                session.add(prompt)
                prompt_ids.append(str(prompt.id))

            await session.commit()

            return {
                "content": [{
                    "type": "text",
                    "text": f"Saved {len(prompt_ids)} prompts to database"
                }]
            }

    except Exception as e:
        return {
            "content": [{
                "type": "text",
                "text": f"Error saving prompts: {str(e)}"
            }],
            "isError": True
        }
```
